name: Panoramic Hillside Trail
name_original: Boucle de la ville jusqu'aux hauteurs
description: Jogging the 10.6-mile City loop to the heights offers a difficult 1 hour and 49 minute workout with 390 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Jogging around the Garonne-Kanal offers diverse terrain, from shaded pathways lined with plane trees to open pastoral countryside. The region features accessible paths, often running alongside the Garonne River, providing scenic views. Runners can explore historical towns, engineering marvels like aqueducts, and dense forests such as Montech Forest. The generally flat and well-maintained paths are suitable for various running abilities. Best jogging routes around the Garonne-Kanal The most popular jogging route is Toulouse – trail loop along the Garonne, an 8.2 miles (13.2 km) trail that takes 1 hour 24 minutes to complete. This route follows the Garonne River, offering a mix of urban and natural scenery. Another top favourite among local runners is The sand fishermen - trail loop starting from Seilh, a moderate 7.0 miles (11.3 km) path. This route winds through the pastoral countryside near Seilh. Local runners also love the Carbonne: Coteaux and Riverside Trail, a 5.8 miles (9.4 km) trail leading through hillsides and along the Garonne River, often completed in about 1 hour 7 minutes. Jogging around the Garonne-Kanal is defined by shaded pathways, pastoral countryside, and historical towns. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult running routes. The routes in Garonne canal are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 600 reviews. More than 56,000 runners have used komoot to explore Garonne canal's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available around the Garonne Canal?
A: There are over 1,600 running routes available around the Garonne Canal, catering to various fitness levels. The komoot community has explored more than 56,000 of these trails.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when running along the Garonne Canal?
A: The Garonne Canal offers diverse terrain, from generally flat and accessible paths to gentle hillsides. You'll find shaded pathways lined with centuries-old plane trees, peaceful pastoral countryside, and scenic riverside views. Some sections, like those near Montech, pass by dense forests, while others offer a mix of urban and natural scenery, especially closer to Toulouse.

Q: Are there running routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easy run?
A: Yes, the Garonne Canal region offers a good selection of easy running routes. Out of over 1,600 trails, more than 180 are classified as easy, making them perfect for beginners or those seeking a relaxed run. An example of an easy route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e978248029/trail-along-the-garonne-and-in-the-ramiers-park-blagnac" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Trail along the Garonne and in the Ramiers park – Blagnac&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails along the Garonne Canal?
A: The running routes around the Garonne Canal are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 680 reviews. Runners often praise the well-maintained, traffic-free paths, the beautiful shaded sections, and the variety of landscapes, from tranquil countryside to historical towns.

Q: When is the best time of year to go running along the Garonne Canal?
A: The Garonne Canal is enjoyable for running throughout much of the year. The shaded pathways provided by the plane trees make it particularly pleasant during warmer months. Early summer is especially beautiful, as the canal banks around Agen are adorned with yellow irises. The generally mild climate of the region allows for comfortable running in spring and autumn as well.

Q: Are there any circular running routes available?
A: Yes, there are several circular running routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e845628497/les-eaux-melees-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Les Eaux Mêlées Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 9.5 km (5.9 mi) trail that offers a scenic round trip. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1001306405/lusignan-grand-panoramic-view-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lusignan-Grand Panoramic View Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 4.8 km (3 mi) moderate route with great vistas.

Q: What interesting landmarks or attractions can I see along the running routes?
A: The Garonne Canal region is rich in historical and engineering marvels. You can run past impressive structures like the magnificent Agen Aqueduct (Pont-Canal d'Agen) and the unique Montech Waterslope. The canal also connects numerous historical towns and villages. Highlights include Moissac, known for its Abbey of St-Pierre (a UNESCO World Heritage site), and Auvillar, recognized as one of France's most beautiful villages. You can also find viewpoints such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4798376" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Pyrenees&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5130997" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Les from Mirador Guardador de Les&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails along the Garonne Canal?
A: Many of the paths along the Garonne Canal are suitable for running with dogs, especially in the more rural and natural sections. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, particularly in populated areas or near wildlife, and to check local regulations for specific areas or nature reserves you plan to visit.

Q: Are there any running routes with significant elevation changes for a more challenging workout?
A: While much of the Garonne Canal is relatively flat, there are routes that offer more significant elevation changes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e931179078/montcorbison-and-tuc-de-letassi-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montcorbison and Tuc de Letassi Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 8.1 km (5 mi) trail with over 540 meters (1,770 ft) of elevation gain, providing a challenging run through hillsides and varied terrain.

Q: Are there options for public transport access to the running trails?
A: Yes, several towns along the Garonne Canal, such as Toulouse and Agen, are well-connected by public transport, making it possible to access various running routes. In urban areas like Toulouse, the Garonne River paths are easily reachable by local transport. For specific trailheads in more rural areas, checking local bus schedules or regional train lines is advisable.

Q: Where can I find parking near the running routes?
A: Parking is generally available in the towns and villages along the Garonne Canal, such as Moissac, Auvillar, Valence d'Agen, and Agen. Many trailheads, especially those starting from urban centers or popular recreational areas, will have designated parking facilities. It's recommended to check specific starting points for parking availability when planning your run.

Q: Are there any routes that combine urban and natural scenery?
A: Absolutely. The Garonne Canal offers a unique blend of urban and natural environments. Routes near Toulouse, such as the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974176478/toulouse-trail-loop-along-the-garonne" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Toulouse – trail loop along the Garonne&lt;/a&gt;, follow the river, providing views of the city on one bank and a wilder, more natural side on the other. This allows runners to experience both the cultural richness of the city and the tranquility of the riverside.
tips: Nice little park under the cool trees

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this jogging route?
faqs.answer: This route is considered difficult for jogging. While the elevation gain is moderate at around 119 meters, the overall distance of nearly 17 kilometers contributes to its challenging rating, especially for a continuous jog.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this jogging loop?
faqs.answer: On average, joggers can expect to complete this loop in approximately 1 hour and 50 minutes. However, individual times may vary based on fitness level and pace.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: As a city loop, the terrain will primarily consist of paved paths, sidewalks, and possibly some park trails. Given its 'difficult' rating and elevation changes, expect some inclines and declines, typical for a route that reaches 'heights' within an urban setting.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or viewpoints along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route features several points of interest. You'll cross the impressive Jacques Chaban-Delmas Bridge, enjoy a View of Bordeaux and the Garonne River, and pass by the historic Cypressat Park Chapel.

faqs.question: Is this jogging route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' rating and nearly 17-kilometer distance, this route might be challenging for absolute beginners. Those new to jogging or longer distances may find it more enjoyable to tackle shorter sections or build up their endurance first.

faqs.question: Is the City loop to the heights dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are permitted on city paths and in parks, usually on a leash. However, specific rules can vary by municipality and park section. It's always best to check local signage, especially in more sensitive areas or during peak times, to ensure your dog is welcome.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this route?
faqs.answer: Spring and autumn typically offer the most pleasant conditions for jogging, with milder temperatures. Summer can be quite warm, especially in the city, so early mornings or late evenings are preferable. Winters are generally mild but can be damp.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to use this jogging route?
faqs.answer: As a city loop utilizing public paths and infrastructure, no specific permits or entrance fees are required to jog this route.

faqs.question: Where can I find parking or access public transport for this route?
faqs.answer: As a city loop, there are likely various public parking options and public transport stops (bus, tram) available throughout the city that can provide access to different points along the route. Specific starting points would offer the most convenient access.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of several other official trails. You'll find yourself on parts of the Super Balade 7 - Balade panoramique des coteaux, ReVE 5 - Bordeaux - Artigues près Bordeaux, and Le Tour de Gironde à Vélo - Étape 6 (Blaye - Bordeaux), among others.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like water fountains or restrooms along the route?
faqs.answer: Being an urban route, you will likely find public water fountains and restrooms in parks or near public buildings along the way. It's advisable to carry your own water, especially during warmer months, and plan stops for facilities as they may not be continuously available.

waypoints.name: View of Bordeaux and the Garonne River
